Fleet sailing against England in 1588

The **Spanish Armada** (often known as **Invincible Armada**, or the **Enterprise of England**, Spanish: *Grande y Felicísima Armada*, lit. 'Great and Most Fortunate Navy') was a [Spanish](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") fleet that sailed from [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") in late May 1588, commanded by [Alonso de Guzmán, Duke of Medina Sidonia](/wiki/Alonso_de_Guzm%C3%A1n_y_Sotomayor,_7th_Duke_of_Medina_Sidonia "Alonso de Guzmán y Sotomayor, 7th Duke of Medina Sidonia"), an aristocrat appointed by [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"). His orders were to sail up the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"), join with the army of [Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma](/wiki/Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma") in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), and escort an invasion force that would land in [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and overthrow [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"). Its purpose was to reinstate [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism_in_England "Catholicism in England") in England, end English support for the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") in the north and prevent attacks by English and Dutch [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") against Spanish interests in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").

The Spanish were opposed by an English fleet based in [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth "Plymouth"). Faster and more manoeuvrable than the larger Spanish [galleons](/wiki/Galleon "Galleon"), its ships were able to attack the armada as it sailed up the Channel. Several subordinates advised Medina Sidonia first to enter [Plymouth Sound](/wiki/Plymouth_Sound "Plymouth Sound") and attack the English fleet before it could leave harbour and then to anchor in [the Solent](/wiki/The_Solent "The Solent") and occupy the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight"), but he refused to deviate from his instructions to join with Parma. Although the armada reached [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais") largely intact, while awaiting communication from Parma, it was attacked at night by English [fire ships](/wiki/Fire_ship "Fire ship") and forced to scatter. The armada suffered further losses in the ensuing Battle of [Gravelines](/wiki/Gravelines "Gravelines") and was in danger of running aground on the Dutch coast when the wind changed, allowing it to escape into the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). Pursued by the English, the Spanish ships returned home via [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). Up to 24 ships were wrecked along the way before the rest managed to get home. Among the factors contributing to the defeat and withdrawal of the armada were bad weather conditions and the better employment of naval guns and battle tactics by the English.

The expedition was the largest engagement of the [undeclared](/wiki/Undeclared_war "Undeclared war") [Anglo-Spanish War](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)"). The following year, England organized a similar large-scale campaign against Spain, known as the "[English Armada](/wiki/English_Armada "English Armada")", and sometimes called the "counter-armada of 1589", which failed. Three further Spanish armadas were sent against England and Ireland in [1596](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Armada "Second Spanish Armada"), [1597](/wiki/Third_Spanish_Armada "Third Spanish Armada"), and [1601](/wiki/Fourth_Spanish_Armada "Fourth Spanish Armada"),[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham1972258–261-23) but these likewise ended in failure.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *[armada](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/armada "wikt:armada")* is from [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *armada*, which is cognate with English *army*. It is originally derived from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *armāta*, the past participle of *armāre*, 'to arm', used in [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") as a noun for *armed force*, *army*, *navy*, *fleet*.[[23]](#cite_note-24) *Armada Española* is still the Spanish term for the modern [Spanish Navy](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

England had been strategically in alliance with Spain for many decades prior to England and Spain entering into war. In the mid to late 15th century, France under [Louis XI](/wiki/Louis_XI "Louis XI") was the strongest power in western Europe. England still had possessions in what today is called Northern France, and Spain was under constant threat. [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") of England therefore formed a strategic relationship with [Ferdinand II](/wiki/Ferdinand_II_of_Aragon "Ferdinand II of Aragon"), of Spain. Whilst the threat from France remained, England and Spain enjoyed many decades of peace which included a number of strategic marriages to retain the alliance. There were many causes of jealousy between the two royal houses over the years, but the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion") were the ultimate cause of the alliance breaking between [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") and [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"), and this led to war between the two countries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By the mid sixteenth century [Habsburg Spain](/wiki/Habsburg_Spain "Habsburg Spain") under Philip II was a dominant political and military power in Europe, with a global empire which became the source of her wealth. It championed the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") cause and its global possessions stretched [from Europe](/wiki/Spanish_Road "Spanish Road"), the [Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas") and to the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). This was expanded further in 1580 when [Philip gained control of Portugal](/wiki/War_of_Portuguese_Succession "War of Portuguese Succession") thus forming the [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union"). Philip became the first monarch who ruled over an [empire upon which the sun did not set](/wiki/The_empire_on_which_the_sun_never_sets "The empire on which the sun never sets"), and he did so from his chambers in the [Escorial Palace](/wiki/El_Escorial "El Escorial") by means of written communication.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker20023-25)

In comparison, England was only a minor European power with no empire and had little influence overseas. During [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII")'s reign, England had gone to war three times against France in alliance with Spain. The last of these conflicts was the [Sieges of Boulogne (1544–1546)](/wiki/Sieges_of_Boulogne_(1544%E2%80%931546) "Sieges of Boulogne (1544–1546)").

Henry VIII began the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") as a political exercise over his desire to divorce his first wife, [Catherine of Aragon](/wiki/Catherine_of_Aragon "Catherine of Aragon"). Over time, England became increasingly aligned with the [Protestant reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") taking place in Europe, especially during the reign of Henry's son, [Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI_of_England "Edward VI of England"). But Edward died childless, and his [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") half-sister [Mary](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England") ascended the throne in 1553. Three years later Mary married Philip II, becoming [queen consort](/wiki/Queen_consort "Queen consort") of Spain. Mary returned England to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"); during her reign more than 260 English Protestants were [burned at the stake](/wiki/Death_by_burning "Death by burning"), earning her the nickname "Bloody Mary".[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaller2006115-26)

In 1557, Philip persuaded Mary to enter into a disastrous war against France. England landed forces in the Low Countries and with the failing support of Spain, won the [Battle of St. Quentin](/wiki/Battle_of_St._Quentin_(1557) "Battle of St. Quentin (1557)"). Though this brought victory for Spain, England had neglected her defenses in France, and France [took Calais in 1558](/wiki/Siege_of_Calais_(1558) "Siege of Calais (1558)"). Thus England lost her last possession in France, which she had held for over 200 years. This was undoubtedly a huge blow to Mary's prestige, who is reported as stating "When I am dead and opened, you shall find 'Calais' lying in my heart." England's wealth further suffered, not just from the cost of the war, but also from the reduced revenues from alum and the Antwerp cloth trade, caused by the loss of the port. The Kingdom of Spain had strengthened its hold on the Low Countries, weakening France with no cost to itself, but at great cost to England. Just before Mary's death, Philip and Mary's half-sister Elizabeth looked to come to an alliance and settlement between England and Spain, and there is evidence even marriage between Philip and Elizabeth was explored, but the question of faith, and the unequal relationship between the two Kingdoms made this extremely unlikely. Spain and England had remained in an alliance that had lasted for over 70 years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Mary died in 1558, succeeded by [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"), a Protestant. Elizabeth re-implemented Edward's reforms. Also, in the eyes of the Catholic Church, Henry had never officially divorced Catherine, making Elizabeth illegitimate. Philip therefore deemed Elizabeth a [heretic](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy") and usurper.

Philip supported plots to have Elizabeth overthrown in favour of her Catholic cousin and [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive"), [Mary, Queen of Scots](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots"). These plans were thwarted in 1566–1567, when Mary was forced to abdicate the crown of Scotland in favour of her son [James VI](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), and then was imprisoned by Elizabeth.

Friction between England and Spain continued. English smugglers traded in Spanish colonies and English privateers seized Spanish ships. Elizabeth even established an [alliance with Morocco](/wiki/Anglo-Moroccan_alliance "Anglo-Moroccan alliance").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first documented proposal for the *Enterprise of England* was made in August 1583. The [Marquis of Santa Cruz](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_de_Baz%C3%A1n,_1st_Marquess_of_Santa_Cruz "Álvaro de Bazán, 1st Marquess of Santa Cruz"), flushed with pride of [his victory in the Azores](/wiki/Battle_of_Vila_Franca_do_Campo "Battle of Vila Franca do Campo"), suggested to Philip II that Spain take advantage of the victory to attack England.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e157–158-27)

In 1585, the [Anglo-Spanish War](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)") began. English privateers attacked Spanish fishing boats on the [Grand Banks](/wiki/Grand_Banks "Grand Banks") and raided Spanish colonies,[[b]](#cite_note-29) and England sent troops to support the [Dutch revolt against Spain](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War").

Also, Elizabeth finally had Mary executed in February 1587, due to constant plots in Mary's name.

In retaliation, Philip planned an expedition to invade England to overthrow Elizabeth, or if the armada was not entirely successful, at least obtain freedom of worship for English Catholics and indemnity for English attacks.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPendrill2002286-30)

> If the Armada is not as successful as we hoped but yet not entirely defeated, then you may offer England peace on the following terms. The first is that in England the free use and exercise of our Holy Catholic faith shall be permitted to all Catholics, native and foreign, and that those that are in exile shall be permitted to return. The second is that all the places in my Netherlands which the English hold shall be restored to me and the third that they shall recompense me for the injury they have done me, my dominions and my subjects, which will amount to an exceeding great sum. With regard the free exercise of Catholicism, you may point out to them that since freedom of worship is permitted to the huguenots of France, there will be no sacrifice of dignity in allowing the same privilege to Catholics in England. - Philip II to the Duke of Parma, April 1588.

If the expedition succeeded, it would end English support for the Dutch and English attacks on Spanish ships and colonies. Philip was supported by [Pope Sixtus V](/wiki/Pope_Sixtus_V "Pope Sixtus V"), who treated the invasion as a [crusade](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades"), with the promise of a subsidy should the Armada make land.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollen1907727–729-31)[[c]](#cite_note-32) Substantial support for the invasion was also expected from English Catholics, including wealthy and influential aristocrats and traders.[[30]](#cite_note-33)

Santa Cruz originally drew up plans in 1586 to invade England from Spain. The requirements proved to be astronomical: 94,222 men, and 556 ships of all sizes to carry them and victuals for eight months. The cost would be more than 1.5 billion *maravedis*, clearly beyond even Spain's resources.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis196062,_63-34) Philip then formed a more realistic plan: a fleet sailing from Spain would transport Parma's army from the Low Countries to England.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis196063-35)

A [raid on Cádiz](/wiki/Singeing_the_King_of_Spain%27s_Beard "Singeing the King of Spain's Beard"), led by privateer Francis Drake in April 1587, captured or destroyed about 30 ships and great quantities of supplies, setting preparations back by a year.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWagner199945-36)[[34]](#cite_note-Shepherd-37) Elizabeth's security chief and spymaster, Sir [Francis Walsingham](/wiki/Francis_Walsingham "Francis Walsingham"), urged her ambassador to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [William Harborne](/wiki/William_Harborne "William Harborne"), to get the Ottomans to distract the Spaniards with fleet manoeuvres,[[35]](#cite_note-38) but there is no evidence for the success of that plan.

Parma was initially consulted by Philip II in 1583.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e157–158-27) Parma stressed that three conditions would need to be met to achieve success; absolute secrecy, secure possession and defense of the Dutch provinces, and keep the French from interfering either with a peace agreement or by sowing division between the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(French) "Catholic League (French)") and the [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020c161–162-39)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e165–166-40) Secrecy could not be maintained which made the enterprise vastly more complicated. Philip ultimately combined Parma's plan with that of Santa Cruz, initially entertaining a triple attack, starting with a diversionary raid on Scotland, while the main armada would capture either the [Isle of Wight](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight "Isle of Wight") or [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton") to establish a safe anchorage in [The Solent](/wiki/The_Solent "The Solent"). Parma would then follow with a large army from the Low Countries crossing the English Channel.

The highly experienced Santa Cruz was appointed commander of the naval force, while Parma would command the invasion force.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e170-41) Unfortunately, Santa Cruz died in February 1588. He was replaced by [the Duke of Medina Sidonia](/wiki/Alonso_de_Guzm%C3%A1n_y_Sotomayor,_7th_Duke_of_Medina_Sidonia "Alonso de Guzmán y Sotomayor, 7th Duke of Medina Sidonia"). While a competent soldier and distinguished administrator, Medina Sidonia had no naval experience. He wrote to Philip expressing grave doubts about the planned campaign, but his message was prevented from reaching the King by courtiers on the grounds that God would ensure the armada's success.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoote2003248–252-42)

## Battle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Battle")]

### Fleet descriptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Fleet descriptions")]

See also: [List of ships of the Spanish Armada](/wiki/List_of_ships_of_the_Spanish_Armada "List of ships of the Spanish Armada")

Prior to the undertaking, Pope Sixtus V allowed Philip to collect crusade taxes and granted his men [indulgences](/wiki/Indulgence "Indulgence"). The blessing of the armada's banner on 25 April 1588, was similar to the ceremony used prior to the [Battle of Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto") in 1571. On 21 July 1588 (N.S), the armada set sail from Lisbon and headed for the English Channel. When it left Lisbon, the fleet was composed of 141 ships,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991113–117-43) with 10,138 sailors and 19,315 soldiers. There were also 1,545 non-combatants (volunteers, officers' servants, friars, artillerists etc.)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis196087–91-44) The fleet carried 1,500 brass guns and 1,000 iron guns.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker199960–63-8) The full body of the fleet took two days to leave port.

The armada was delayed by bad weather. Storms in the [Bay of Biscay](/wiki/Bay_of_Biscay "Bay of Biscay") along the [Galician](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)") coast forced four galleys commanded by Captain [Diego de Medrano](/wiki/Diego_de_Medrano "Diego de Medrano") and one galleon to turn back, and other ships had to put in to [A Coruña](/wiki/A_Coru%C3%B1a "A Coruña") for repairs, leaving 137 ships that sailed for the English Channel.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991116-45) Nearly half of the ships were not built as warships and were used for duties such as scouting and dispatch work, or for carrying supplies, animals and troops.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly196312–13-46) The armada included 24 purpose-built warships, 44 armed merchantmen, 38 auxiliary vessels and 34 supply ships.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991117-6)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly196312–13-46)

In the Spanish Netherlands, Parma had mustered a polyglot army of 60,583 soldiers; Spanish, Italians, Burgundians, Irish, Scottish, Walloon and German, with 3650 cavalry.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis196093-47) He ordered hundreds of flyboats to be built to carry them across the channel[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker199994-10)[[d]](#cite_note-49) while awaiting the arrival of the armada. Since the element of surprise was long gone,[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e164,_167,_199–200-50) the new plan was to use the cover of the warships to convey the army on barges to a place near London. In all, 55,000 men were to have been mustered, a huge army for that time. On the day the armada set sail, Elizabeth's ambassador in the Netherlands, [Valentine Dale](/wiki/Valentine_Dale "Valentine Dale"), met Parma's representatives in peace negotiations.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e206–207-51) The English made a vain effort to intercept the armada in the [Bay of Biscay](/wiki/Bay_of_Biscay "Bay of Biscay"). On 6 July, negotiations were abandoned,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e225-52) and the English fleet stood prepared, if ill-supplied, at Plymouth, awaiting news of Spanish movements.

Only 122 ships from the Spanish fleet entered the Channel; the four [galleys](/wiki/Galley "Galley"), one [nao](/wiki/Carrack "Carrack"), five [pataches](/wiki/Patache "Patache") and the 10 Portuguese [caravels](/wiki/Caravel "Caravel") had left the fleet before the first encounter with the English fleet. An additional 5 pataches, dispatched to deliver messages to Parma, should be deducted which brings the number to 117 Spanish ships facing the roughly 226-strong English fleet.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991114–116-53) The Spanish fleet outgunned that of the English with 50% more available firepower than the English.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999185-54) The English fleet consisted of the 34 ships of the Royal Fleet, 21 of which were galleons of 200 to 400 tons, and 163 other ships, 30 of which were of 200 to 400 tons and carried up to 42 guns each. Twelve of the ships were privateers owned by [Lord Howard of Effingham](/wiki/Charles_Howard,_1st_Earl_of_Nottingham "Charles Howard, 1st Earl of Nottingham"), [John Hawkins](/wiki/John_Hawkins_(naval_commander) "John Hawkins (naval commander)") and Sir Francis Drake.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999185-54)

In the beginning of June, Parma had sent Captain Moresin with some pilots to Admiral Sedonia. Upon Moresin's return on 22 June, the report he made to Parma caused him distress. Medina Sedonia was under the impression that Parma could simply sail out into the channel with his barges filled with troops.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e218–219-55) Parma had continually informed the king that his passage to the channel was blocked by English and Dutch ships, and the only way he could bring his boats out was if the armada cleared the blockade.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e220-56)

The fleet was sighted in England on 29 July (N.S), when it appeared off [the Lizard](/wiki/The_Lizard "The Lizard") in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"). The news was conveyed to London by a system of [beacons](/wiki/Beacon "Beacon") that had been constructed along the south coast. The same day the English fleet was trapped in Plymouth Harbour by the incoming tide. The Spanish convened a [council of war](/wiki/Council_of_war "Council of war"), where it was proposed to ride into the harbour on the tide and incapacitate the defending ships at anchor.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1960119-57) From Plymouth Harbour the Spanish would attack England, but Philip explicitly forbade Medina Sidonia from engaging, leaving the armada to sail on to the east and towards the Isle of Wight. As the tide turned, 55 English ships set out to confront the armada from Plymouth under the command of Lord Howard of Effingham, with Sir Francis Drake as vice admiral. The [rear admiral](/wiki/Rear_admiral "Rear admiral") was John Hawkins.

### Action off Plymouth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Action off Plymouth")]

On 30 July, the English fleet was off [Eddystone Rocks](/wiki/Eddystone_Rocks "Eddystone Rocks") with the armada upwind to the west. To execute its attack, the English [tacked](/wiki/Tacking_(sailing) "Tacking (sailing)") upwind of the armada, thus gaining the [weather gage](/wiki/Weather_gage "Weather gage"), a significant advantage. At daybreak on 31 July, the English fleet engaged the armada off Plymouth near the Eddystone Rocks. The armada was in a crescent-shaped defensive formation, convex towards the east. The galleons and great ships were concentrated in the centre and at the tips of the crescent's horns, giving cover to the transports and supply ships in between. Opposing them, the English were in two sections, with Drake to the north in [*Revenge*](/wiki/English_ship_Revenge_(1577) "English ship Revenge (1577)") with 11 ships, and Howard to the south in [*Ark Royal*](/wiki/English_ship_Ark_Royal_(1587) "English ship Ark Royal (1587)") with the bulk of the fleet.

Given the Spanish advantage in close-quarter fighting, the English kept beyond grappling range and bombarded the Spanish ships from a distance with cannon fire. The distance was too great for the manoeuvre to be effective and, at the end of the first day's fighting neither fleet had lost a ship in action. The English caught up with the Spanish fleet after a day of sailing.

### Actions off Portland Bill and Isle of Wight

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Actions off Portland Bill and Isle of Wight")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Vald%C3%A9s_surrenders_to_Francis_Drake_aboard_Revenge.jpg/250px-Vald%C3%A9s_surrenders_to_Francis_Drake_aboard_Revenge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vald%C3%A9s_surrenders_to_Francis_Drake_aboard_Revenge.jpg)

*The Surrender of Pedro de Valdés* (commander of the Squadron of Andalusia) to Francis Drake aboard the *Revenge* during the attack of the Spanish Armada, 1588, by [John Seymour Lucas](/wiki/John_Seymour_Lucas "John Seymour Lucas")

The English fleet and the armada engaged once more on 1 August, off [Portland](/wiki/Isle_of_Portland "Isle of Portland"). A change of wind gave the Spanish the [weather gage](/wiki/Weather_gage "Weather gage"), and they sought to close with the English, but were foiled by the smaller ships' greater manoeuvrability. While the Spanish center manoeuvred to support the *Santa Ana*, the [*Nuestra Señora del Rosario*](/wiki/Spanish_ship_Nuestra_Se%C3%B1ora_del_Rosario_(1587) "Spanish ship Nuestra Señora del Rosario (1587)") collided with a number of ships, losing her [bowsprit](/wiki/Bowsprit "Bowsprit") and setting in motion a series of mishaps. She began to drift, and was taken off by the current in the opposite direction to the fleet and closer to the English. Drake in the *Revenge* sailed to the *Rosario* during the night and she was taken in action; Admiral [Pedro de Valdés](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_de_Vald%C3%A9s&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pedro de Valdés (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pedro_de_Vald%C3%A9s "es:Pedro de Valdés")] (commander of the Squadron of Andalusia) surrendered along with his entire crew. On board, the English seized supplies of much-needed [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") and 50,000 gold [ducats](/wiki/Ducat "Ducat").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKonstam2009150-58) Drake had been guiding the English fleet by means of a lantern, which he snuffed out to slip away from the Spanish ships, causing the rest of his fleet to become scattered and disarrayed by dawn. At one point, Howard formed his ships into a [line of battle](/wiki/Line_of_battle "Line of battle") to attack at close range, bringing all his guns to bear, but he did not follow through with the manoeuvre and little was achieved. During a lull in battle, *San Salvador*'s gunpowder [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(artillery)#Naval_magazines "Magazine (artillery)") exploded, perhaps as a result of sabotage by a disgruntled gunner,[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1960129-59) setting a portion of the ship on fire. The Spanish attempted to [scuttle](/wiki/Scuttling "Scuttling") the ship, but this failed when the *Golden Hind* came up. The Spanish evacuated the vessel and the *Golden Hind* promptly captured her.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKonstam2009151-60)

If the armada could create a temporary base in the protected waters of the Solent, the strait separating the Isle of Wight from the English mainland, it could wait there for word from Parma's army; Parma did not get news of this until 6 August.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e223-61) However, in a full-scale attack, the English fleet broke into four groups with [Martin Frobisher](/wiki/Martin_Frobisher "Martin Frobisher") of the ship [*Aid*](/wiki/English_ship_Aid_(1562) "English ship Aid (1562)") given command over a squadron, and Drake coming with a large force from the south. Medina Sidonia sent reinforcements south and ordered the Armada back to open sea to avoid the [Owers shoals](/wiki/Cymenshore "Cymenshore").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcdermott2005260-62) There were no other secure harbours further east along England's south coast, so the Armada was compelled to make for [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais"), without being able to wait for word of Parma's army.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Starting on 1 August, Sidonia began sending Parma messages detailing his position and movements. However, couriers landed on the French shore or despatched in small vessels could make their way to Parma little faster than the armada itself. It was not until 5 August that Parma received the first report from the Admiral.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e221-63)

### Fireships at Calais

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Fireships at Calais")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Justinus_van_Nassau_1559-1631.jpg/250px-Justinus_van_Nassau_1559-1631.jpg)](/wiki/File:Justinus_van_Nassau_1559-1631.jpg)

[Justinus van Nassau](/wiki/Justinus_van_Nassau "Justinus van Nassau")

On 7 August, the armada anchored off Calais in a tightly packed defensive crescent formation,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodgers2004263–269-64) not far from [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk") (Parma only learned of this on that same afternoon)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e224-65) where Parma's army, reduced by disease to 16,000, was expected to be waiting, ready to join the fleet in barges sent from ports along the Flemish coast. An essential element of the plan of invasion, as it was eventually implemented, was the transportation of a large part of Parma's [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders") as the main invasion force in unarmed barges across the English Channel. These barges would be protected by the large ships of the armada. However, to get to the armada, they would have to cross the zone dominated by the Dutch navy, where the armada could not go due to the ongoing [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") with the Dutch Republic. This problem seems to have been overlooked by the armada's commanders, but it was insurmountable. Communication was more difficult than anticipated, and word came too late that Parma's army had yet to be equipped with sufficient transport or to be assembled in the port, a process that would take at least six days.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e225-52) As Medina Sidonia waited at anchor, Dunkirk was [blockaded](/wiki/Blockade "Blockade") by a Dutch fleet of 30 flyboats under Lieutenant-Admiral [Justinus van Nassau](/wiki/Justinus_van_Nassau "Justinus van Nassau").[[62]](#cite_note-66) The Dutch flyboats mainly operated in the shallow waters off [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland") and [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") where larger warships with a deeper draught, like the Spanish and English galleons, could not safely enter. Parma expected the armada to send its light [pataches](/wiki/Patache "Patache") to drive away the Dutch, but Medina Sidonia would not send them because he feared he would need these ships for his own protection. There was no deep-water port where the fleet might shelter, which had been acknowledged as a major difficulty for the expedition, and the Spanish found themselves vulnerable as night drew on.

The Dutch enjoyed an unchallenged naval advantage in these waters, even though their navy was inferior in naval armament. Because Medina Sidonia did not attempt to break the Dutch blockade and Parma would not risk attempting the passage unescorted, the Army of Flanders escaped the trap that Van Nassau had in mind for them.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsraelParker1991349–351-67)[[64]](#cite_note-68)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Late on 7 August, Howard was reinforced by a squadron under Lord Edward Seymour and [William Wynter](/wiki/William_Wynter "William Wynter"), which had been stationed in [the Downs](/wiki/The_Downs_(ship_anchorage) "The Downs (ship anchorage)") as a reinforcement for the Dutch should Parma make any independent move. Their arrival gave Howard a total of 140 ships. He also received a small amount of powder and shot, which the Earl of Sussex had collected from fortresses and garrisons on the South Coast, and some victuals.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1960164,_166-69)

The wind and currents were favourable for an attempt to break the armada's formation by sending fireships against it. Walsingham had already sent orders to Dover that fishing smacks and faggots and [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(resin) "Pitch (resin)") were to be collected for this purpose. However, the English commanders felt that they could not wait for proper fireships and therefore sacrificed eight of their own warships. Drake, who was a substantial shipowner, offered one of his own ships, the 200-ton "Thomas". Hawkins also offered one of his ships, the 150-ton "Bark Bond". Six other ships, of between 90 and 200 tons, were volunteered. These ships were filled with whatever pitch, [brimstone](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") and [tar](/wiki/Tar "Tar") was immediately available. Because of the haste, the loaded guns and stores were left aboard.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1960168–169-70)

In the middle of the night of 7–8 August, the English set these fireships alight and cast them downwind among the closely anchored vessels of the armada. The Spanish feared that these uncommonly large fireships were "[hellburners](/wiki/Hellburners "Hellburners")",[[67]](#cite_note-Drizzle-71)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] specialised fireships filled with large gunpowder charges that had been used to deadly effect at the [Siege of Antwerp](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp"). Three were intercepted by pataches and towed away,[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991119-72) but the remainder bore down on the fleet. Medina Sidonia's [flagship](/wiki/Flagship "Flagship") and the principal warships held their positions, but the rest of the fleet cut their anchor cables and scattered in confusion. No Spanish ships were burnt, but the crescent formation had been broken, and the fleet found itself too far [leeward](/wiki/Windward_and_leeward "Windward and leeward") of Calais in the rising southwesterly wind to recover its position. Another loss, the effect of which would not be felt until later, was almost every anchor the Armada's ships possessed.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1960171-73) The English closed in for battle. Parma learned of this the following day.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e226–227-74)

### Battle of Gravelines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Battle of Gravelines")]

"Battle of Gravelines (1588)" redirects here. For the battle in the Italian War of 1551–1559, see [Battle of Gravelines (1558)](/wiki/Battle_of_Gravelines_(1558) "Battle of Gravelines (1558)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Loutherbourg-Spanish_Armada.jpg/250px-Loutherbourg-Spanish_Armada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Loutherbourg-Spanish_Armada.jpg)

*[Defeat of the Spanish Armada, 8 August 1588](/wiki/Defeat_of_the_Spanish_Armada "Defeat of the Spanish Armada")*, painted by [Philip James de Loutherbourg](/wiki/Philip_James_de_Loutherbourg "Philip James de Loutherbourg") (1796)

The small port of [Gravelines](/wiki/Gravelines "Gravelines") was part of Flanders in the Spanish Netherlands close to the border with France, and was the closest Spanish territory to England.

Before dawn on 8 August, Medina Sidonia struggled to regather his fleet after the fireships scattered it, and was reluctant to sail further east than Gravelines, knowing the danger of running aground on the shoals off Flanders, from which his Dutch enemies had removed the [sea marks](/wiki/Sea_mark "Sea mark"). The English learned of the armada's weaknesses during the skirmishes in the English Channel, and concluded it was possible to close in to within 100 yards (90 m) to be able to penetrate the oak hulls of the Spanish warships. They had spent most of their gunpowder in the first engagements and had, after the Isle of Wight, been forced to conserve their heavy shot and powder for an anticipated attack near Gravelines. During all the engagements, the Spanish heavy guns could not easily be reloaded because of their close spacing and the quantities of supplies stowed between decks, as Drake had discovered on capturing the *Nuestra Señora del Rosario* in the Channel.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoote2003259-75) Instead, the Spanish gunners fired once and then transferred to their main task, which was to [board](/wiki/Naval_boarding "Naval boarding") enemy ships, as had been the practice in naval warfare at the time. Evidence from Armada wrecks in Ireland shows that much of the fleet's ammunition was unused.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999189–190-76) Their determination to fight by boarding, rather than employing cannon fire at a distance, proved a disadvantage for the Spanish. The manoeuver had been effective in the battles of [Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto") and [Ponta Delgada](/wiki/Battle_of_Vila_Franca_do_Campo "Battle of Vila Franca do Campo") earlier in the decade, but the English were aware of it and sought to avoid it by keeping their distance.

While Medina Sidonia was gathering the armada ships together into their traditional crescent formation the English fleet moved in, and at dawn the flagship with four other ships found themselves facing the entire English fleet.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999190–191-77)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991119-72) The English provoked Spanish fire while staying out of range. The English then closed, firing damaging broadsides into the enemy ships, all the while maintaining a windward position, so the [heeling](/wiki/Heel_(sailing) "Heel (sailing)") armada hulls were exposed to damage below the water line when they changed course later. Many of the Spanish gunners were killed or wounded by the English broadsides, and the task of manning the cannon often fell to foot soldiers who did not know how to operate them. The ships were close enough for sailors on the upper decks of the English and Spanish ships to exchange musket fire. A couple of hours into the battle, a few more armada warships closed in to form wings on either side of the five ships already under attack.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999188-78) After eight hours, the English ships began to run out of ammunition, and some gunners began loading objects such as chains into their cannons. Around 4 pm, the English fired their last shots and pulled back.[[75]](#cite_note-79)

Five Spanish and Portuguese ships were lost: the 605 ton *Maria Juan*, a [carrack](/wiki/Carrack "Carrack") which had been part of Don Diego Flores de Valdes' Castile Squadron which had attempted to surrender to Captain [Robert Crosse](/wiki/Robert_Crosse_(MP) "Robert Crosse (MP)") of the *Hope*, sank off [Blankenberge](/wiki/Blankenberge "Blankenberge") with the loss of 275 men – the Spanish only managing to rescue a single boatload of survivors.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2011453-80) The [galleass](/wiki/Galleass "Galleass") *San Lorenzo*, the flagship of Don [Hugo de Moncada](/wiki/Hugo_of_Moncada_i_Gralla "Hugo of Moncada i Gralla") which had been holed below the waterline, was forced to run aground at Calais to avoid sinking. On sight of this, Admiral Howard ordered a flotilla of ship's boats to carry her by boarding. Moncada was killed during an exchange of small arms fire, a shot to his head from an arquebus. The ship was then taken after murderous fighting between the crew, galley slaves and the English. The French meanwhile could do little except to watch as the ship was plundered, but they opened fire to ward off the English who quickly left to join the rest of the fight.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly2005328–329-81) The next day, the severely crippled galleon *San Mateo* ran aground in between Sluis and Ostend; it was taken by a combination of Dutch ships and English troops led by [Francis Vere](/wiki/Francis_Vere "Francis Vere").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStedall202241-82) The captain, Don Diego Pimmental, surrendered along with the survivors of his crew. Later that day, the equally crippled *San Felipe*, commanded by Maestre de Campo Don Fransico de Toledo, drifted away as she was sinking and ran aground on the island of [Walcheren](/wiki/Walcheren "Walcheren"). The English troops sortied from [Flushing](/wiki/Vlissingen "Vlissingen") to the wreck, attacked the stricken vessel, and took the crew prisoners. A Dutch force of flyboats led by [Justinus van Nassau](/wiki/Justinus_van_Nassau "Justinus van Nassau") then took possession of the ship. A pinnace was also run aground by her crew to prevent her from sinking.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999190–191-77)

Many other Spanish ships were severely damaged, especially the Portuguese and some Spanish Atlantic-class galleons, including some Neapolitan galleys, which bore the brunt of the fighting during the early hours of the battle:[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991120-83) the Spanish *Nuestra Señora del Rosario*, *San Salvador*, and *La María Juan*; the Neapolitan *San Lorenzo*; and the Portuguese *São Mateus* and *São Filipe*. The Spanish plan to join with Parma's army had been frustrated.

### Elizabeth's Tilbury speech

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Elizabeth's Tilbury speech")]

Main article: [Speech to the Troops at Tilbury](/wiki/Speech_to_the_Troops_at_Tilbury "Speech to the Troops at Tilbury")

Because of the potential invasion from the Netherlands, [Robert Dudley, Earl of Leicester](/wiki/Robert_Dudley,_1st_Earl_of_Leicester "Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester") assembled a force of 4,500 militia at [West Tilbury](/wiki/West_Tilbury "West Tilbury"), Essex, to defend the [Thames Estuary](/wiki/Thames_Estuary "Thames Estuary") against any incursion up-river towards London. The result of the English fireship attack and the sea battle of Gravelines had not yet reached England, so Elizabeth went to Tilbury on 18 August to review her forces, arriving on horseback in ceremonial armor to imply to the militia that she was prepared to lead them in the ensuing battle.[[e]](#cite_note-85) She gave them her royal address, which survives in at least six slightly different versions.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuy2016119-86) One version is as follows:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Elizabeth_I_%28Armada_Portrait%29.jpg/330px-Elizabeth_I_%28Armada_Portrait%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elizabeth_I_(Armada_Portrait).jpg)

Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"), the [Armada Portrait](/wiki/Armada_Portrait "Armada Portrait") at [Woburn Abbey](/wiki/Woburn_Abbey "Woburn Abbey"), Bedfordshire

> My loving people, we have been persuaded by some that are careful of our safety, to take heed how we commit ourselves to armed multitudes for fear of treachery; but, I do assure you, I do not desire to live to distrust my faithful and loving people. Let tyrants fear, I have always so behaved myself, that under God I have placed my chiefest strength and safeguard in the loyal hearts and goodwill of my subjects; and, therefore, I am come amongst you as you see at this time, not for my recreation and disport, but being resolved, in the midst and heat of battle, to live or die amongst you all – to lay down for my God, and for my kingdoms, and for my people, my honour and my blood even in the dust. I know I have the body of a weak and feeble woman; but I have the heart and stomach of a king – and of a King of England too, and think foul scorn that Parma or Spain, or any prince of Europe, should dare to invade the borders of my realm; to which, rather than any dishonour should grow by me, I myself will take up arms – I myself will be your general, judge, and rewarder of every one of your virtues in the field. I know already, for your forwardness, you have deserved rewards and crowns, and, we do assure you, on the word of a prince, they shall be duly paid you. In the mean time, my lieutenant general shall be in my stead, than whom never prince commanded a more noble or worthy subject; not doubting but by your obedience to my general, by your concord in the camp, and your valour in the field, we shall shortly have a famous victory over those enemies of my God, of my kingdom, and of my people.

— [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I")[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson1976320-87)

### Armada in Scotland and Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Armada in Scotland and Ireland")]

Main article: [Spanish Armada in Ireland](/wiki/Spanish_Armada_in_Ireland "Spanish Armada in Ireland")

On the day after the battle at Gravelines, the disorganized and unmanouvreable Spanish fleet was at risk of running onto the sands of Zeeland because of the prevailing wind. The wind then changed to the south, enabling the fleet to sail north. The English ships under Howard pursued to prevent any landing on English soil, although by this time his ships were almost out of shot. On 12 August, Howard called a halt to the pursuit at about the latitude of the [Firth of Forth](/wiki/Firth_of_Forth "Firth of Forth") off Scotland. The only option left to the Spanish ships was to return to Spain by sailing round the north of Scotland and home via the Atlantic or the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"). As the Spanish fleet rounded Scotland on 20 August, it consisted of 110 vessels and most made it around.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991120-83) The *[San Juan de Sicilia](/wiki/San_Juan_de_Sicilia "San Juan de Sicilia")*, heavily damaged during the Gravelines engagement, had struggled North and limped into [Tobermory](/wiki/Tobermory,_Mull "Tobermory, Mull") bay on the [Isle of Mull](/wiki/Isle_of_Mull "Isle of Mull") on 23 September, but was later destroyed by an English agent sent by Francis Walsingham with most of the crew on board.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999244-90)

The Spanish ships were beginning to show wear from the long voyage, and some were kept together by strengthening their damaged hulls with cables. Supplies of food and water ran short. The intention would have been to keep to the west of the coasts of Scotland and Ireland, seeking the relative safety of the open sea. There being no way of accurately measuring [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude"), the Spanish were not aware that the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") was carrying them north and east as they tried to move west, and they eventually turned south much closer to the coast than they thought. Off Scotland and Ireland, the fleet ran into a series of powerful westerly winds which drove many of the damaged ships further towards the [lee shore](/wiki/Lee_shore "Lee shore"). Because so many anchors had been abandoned during the escape from the English fireships off Calais, many of the ships were incapable of securing shelter as the fleet reached the coast of Ireland and were driven onto the rocks; local inhabitants looted the ships. The late sixteenth century and especially 1588 was marked by unusually strong North Atlantic storms, perhaps associated with a high accumulation of polar ice off the coast of [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), a feature of the "[Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age")".[[86]](#cite_note-91) More ships and sailors were lost to cold and stormy weather than in direct combat.

Most of the 28 ships lost in the storms were along the jagged steep rocks of the western coast of Ireland.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991120-83) About 5,000 men died by drowning, starvation and slaughter by local inhabitants after their ships were driven ashore on the west coasts of Scotland and Ireland. The English Lord Deputy [William FitzWilliam](/wiki/William_FitzWilliam_(Lord_Deputy) "William FitzWilliam (Lord Deputy)") ordered the English soldiers in Ireland to kill any Spanish prisoners, which was done on several occasions instead of asking for ransom as was common during that period.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly2005369-92) Reports of the passage of the remnants of the Spanish Armada around Ireland abound with onerous accounts of hardships and survival.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChurchill1956130-93) One of the costliest wrecks was that of the [galleass](/wiki/Galleass "Galleass") *[La Girona](/wiki/Girona_(ship) "Girona (ship)")*, which was driven on to [Lacada Point](/w/index.php?title=Lacada_Point&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lacada Point (page does not exist)") in [County Antrim](/wiki/County_Antrim "County Antrim") on the night of 26 October. Of the estimated 1,300 people on board, there were nine survivors. 260 bodies washed ashore, including [Alonso Martínez de Leiva](/w/index.php?title=Alonso_Mart%C3%ADnez_de_Leiva&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alonso Martínez de Leiva (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alonso_Mart%C3%ADnez_de_Leiva "es:Alonso Martínez de Leiva")], knight and member of the Council of Thirteen (*trece*) of the [Order of Santiago](/wiki/Order_of_Santiago "Order of Santiago"). Captain [Francisco de Cuéllar](/wiki/Francisco_de_Cuellar "Francisco de Cuellar") was wrecked on the coast of Ireland and gave a remarkable account of his experiences in the fleet, [on the run in Ireland](/wiki/Francisco_de_Cuellar#O'Rourke's_country "Francisco de Cuellar"), defeat of an English army [besieging Rosclogher castle](/wiki/Francisco_de_Cuellar#Siege_at_Rosclogher "Francisco de Cuellar"), flight through Scotland, surviving a second shipwreck and [ultimate return to Spain](/wiki/Francisco_de_Cuellar#Escape "Francisco de Cuellar").[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernández-Armesto1988255–263-94)[[90]](#cite_note-95)

### Return to Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Return to Spain")]

Continental Europe had been anxiously awaiting news of the armada all summer. The Spanish postmaster and Spanish agents in Rome promoted reports of Spanish victory in hopes of convincing [Pope Sixtus V](/wiki/Pope_Sixtus_V "Pope Sixtus V") to release his promise of one million [ducats](/wiki/Ducat "Ducat") upon landing of troops. In France, the Spanish and English ambassadors promoted contradictory narratives in the press, and a Spanish victory was incorrectly celebrated in Paris, Prague, and Venice. It was not until late August that reliable reports of the Spanish defeat arrived in major cities and were widely believed.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPettegree2015153–154-96)

The first rumours of a setback for the armada began to reach Spain when news of the English fireships breaking the Spanish formation at Calais was received, but this was disbelieved. The King noted "I hope God has not permitted so much evil". Nothing was heard for nearly two weeks and it was not until 21 September that the first of the ships of the armada began to arrive into Spain – the first of eight entered into Coruña which included Medina Sidonia's *San Martin*. Over the next few days Diego Flores took 22 more into [Laredo](/wiki/Laredo,_Spain "Laredo, Spain") harbour and Miguel de Oquendo brought five more into the port of [Guipuzcoa](/wiki/Guipuzcoa "Guipuzcoa").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999239-97)

After Medina Sidonia entered Coruña, and following the death of Admiral Miguel de Oquendo, Captain [Diego de Medrano](/wiki/Diego_de_Medrano "Diego de Medrano") was appointed interim Admiral to command the remaining armada back to Spain.[[93]](#cite_note-98) By mid-October it was becoming more apparent for the Spanish that few if any more of the remaining missing ships of the armada would return. Even in November three months after the battles through the Channel, a few Spanish ships were still attempting the journey home.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarratt2006172-99) One of the last, the Spanish hospital ship *San Pedro El Mayor*, carrying some 200 sick and wounded survivors, came into [Hope Cove](/wiki/Hope_Cove "Hope Cove") in [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon") on 7 November, the commander trying to find a suitable place to ground her. The crew were taken prisoner and the sick were treated in [Bodmin](/wiki/Bodmin "Bodmin") and [Plympton](/wiki/Plympton "Plympton").[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2011494-100)[[96]](#cite_note-101)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e3/Routes_of_the_Spanish_Armada.svg/250px-Routes_of_the_Spanish_Armada.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Routes_of_the_Spanish_Armada.svg)

Route taken by the Spanish Armada

After the Duke of Parma was certain that the armada had sailed away from the coast of Flanders and his participation in the invasion project was no longer feasible, he ordered his soldiers to disembark so as to avoid an epidemic of disease.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e229-102) He then assembled his council of war to discuss what endeavours his forces could be used for before the onset of winter. In late September, he divided them into three groups; one was sent to the Rhine, one was to remain in the coastal region and one was led by Parma himself against [Bergen-op-Zoom](/wiki/Bergen-op-Zoom "Bergen-op-Zoom"). There, in November, he was [repelled with heavy losses](/wiki/Siege_of_Bergen_op_Zoom_(1588) "Siege of Bergen op Zoom (1588)") by the Anglo-Dutch garrison, which, in combination with poor weather, forced him to abandon the siege.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack2005111-103)[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020e242–243-104)An attempt to take the Dutch-held island of [Tholen](/wiki/Tholen "Tholen") was also repelled. From the armada campaign to Bergen, Parma's forces had lost some 10,000 men killed or dead from disease.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWernham198448-105)

### England and the Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: England and the Netherlands")]

Even though the Spanish Armada had failed to invade England it had demonstrated its feasibility and shown that the British Isles overall were vulnerable to attack.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernández-Armesto1988268–269-106)

The day after her Tilbury speech, Elizabeth ordered the army disbanded, the camp at Tilbury dissolved five days later, then discharged the navy, sending them home without pay.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutchinson2013215–216-107)[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999254-108) All the while, the costs of this defensive effort were mounting – the total was nearly £400,000[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999254-108) – and measures were put in motion to mitigate it. [Typhus](/wiki/Typhus "Typhus"), [scurvy](/wiki/Scurvy "Scurvy") and [dysentery](/wiki/Dysentery "Dysentery") swept through the crews and many died of disease and starvation after landing at [Margate](/wiki/Margate "Margate"). For instance, of the [*Bonaventure*](/wiki/English_ship_Bonaventure_(1567) "English ship Bonaventure (1567)")'s crew of 500, more than 200 had died[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly2005216-109) and the [*Elizabeth Jonas*](/wiki/English_ship_Elizabeth_Jonas "English ship Elizabeth Jonas") had just one living from the crew that it had sailed with.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutchinson2013216-110) Howard wrote to [Lord Burghley](/wiki/William_Cecil,_1st_Baron_Burghley "William Cecil, 1st Baron Burghley"), Elizabeth's [Lord High Treasurer](/wiki/Lord_High_Treasurer "Lord High Treasurer"), "it would grieve any man's heart to see them that have served so valiantly die so miserably". Hawkins also weighed in and accused Burghley "that by death, by discharging of sick men, and such like, that there may be spared something in the general pay".[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaughton189496,_163-111) The men had to rely on the charity of their officers, and Howard set an example by doing what he could out of his own purse to help the sailors. Nevertheless, upwards of 3,000 perished.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBicheno2012262-14)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuy2016144–148-112) As a result, the [Chatham Chest](/wiki/Chatham_Chest "Chatham Chest") was set up, its purpose to help pay pensions to disabled seamen.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly2005259-113)[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999254-108)

It took some time for the scale of the victory to be realized as news began to filter through by the end of August and beginning of September.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999237-114) As a result, a number of thanksgiving services were held at cathedrals and churches throughout England. At [St Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/Old_St_Paul%27s_Cathedral "Old St Paul's Cathedral") in [London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") a series of thanksgiving services took place, the first on 30 August where a [sermon](/wiki/Sermon "Sermon") was preached, followed by another on 18 September. As news of the full scale of the disaster came through, and also news of England's victory against Parma at Bergen-op-Zoom, a bigger [national service of thanksgiving](/wiki/National_service_of_thanksgiving "National service of thanksgiving") took place on 29 November.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2011526–527-115) A second and final thanksgiving took place five days later which saw a royal procession of the Queen in a chariot through the streets of London.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHadfield1964198-116) Twelve Spanish standards and other trophies which had been captured from the ships of the armada decorated the choir of St Paul during the huge service.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker199938-117)

The captured Spanish galleons *Nuestra Señora del Rosario* and the *San Salvador* were studied by the English. The *San Salvador* became known as the "Great Spaniard" but was lost in a wreck in November 1588 off [Studland](/wiki/Studland "Studland"). *Nuestra Señora del Rosario* was brought to [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth,_Devon "Dartmouth, Devon"). The 397 crew were taken to [Torre Abbey](/wiki/Torre_Abbey "Torre Abbey") near [Torquay](/wiki/Torquay "Torquay") where they were held prisoner in a barn (today called the 'Spanish Barn') and spared execution.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin198838-118) The *Rosario* was later sent to [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham_Dockyard "Chatham Dockyard") where she was [dry docked](/wiki/Dry_dock "Dry dock") and eventually sunk to support a [wharf](/wiki/Wharf "Wharf").[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin198836-119) Pedro de Valdés was held prisoner in the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London") for five years, until his ransom was paid by his family for his release back to Spain. He was not blamed for the loss of his ship and was appointed colonial governor of [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") from 1602 to 1608.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker199987–92-120)

The Dutch also celebrated the victory and their artists were quick to take on commemorative medals and paintings which were soon circulated within the year. The wrecked Spanish galleons *San Mateo* and *San Felipe* which had run aground were both found to be riddled with holes by cannonballs that had struck below the waterline. Both ships were too damaged to be salvaged and were therefore broken up; the cannons were used by the Dutch in nearby fortresses. The main-topmast rigging banner from the *San Matteo*, part of which depicts Christ on the cross, was taken, hung and displayed in the choir of [St Peters Church](/wiki/Pieterskerk,_Leiden "Pieterskerk, Leiden") in [Leiden](/wiki/Leiden "Leiden"). It now resides in the [Museum De Lakenhal](/wiki/Museum_De_Lakenhal "Museum De Lakenhal").[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2011454-121)

### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Spain")]

The news of the disaster brought shock and despair and the nation went into mourning.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotley1888534–536-122) Its defeat was even more devastating because hopes of its success had been raised by false rumours. These included Drake and Howard being taken prisoner, the Isle of Wight and Plymouth taken and Parma's army even approaching London.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutchinson2013205-123) The King took the news hard and shut himself away for days. The daily business of government was also brought to an abrupt halt. The King is claimed to have said: "I sent the armada against men, not God's winds and waves".[[119]](#cite_note-124) News of the loss of *La Girona* bore more despair for Philip: not only De Leiva but also his[*[ambiguous](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] followers from almost every noble house in Spain had drowned with it.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2011480-125)

The number of ships lost has been debated. A detailed study by Spanish naval historian [Fernandez Duro](/wiki/Ces%C3%A1reo_Fern%C3%A1ndez_Duro "Cesáreo Fernández Duro") in the mid-1880s claimed that 63 in total were lost.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernández_Duro1884140-126) Historian [José Luis Casado Soto](/w/index.php?title=Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Casado_Soto&action=edit&redlink=1 "José Luis Casado Soto (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Luis_Casado_Soto "es:José Luis Casado Soto")] examined the fate of each ship creating individual dossiers and claimed that 35 ships were lost.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991120–122-127) In addition it was noted that of the 122 armada ships that entered the English Channel, 87 returned from their voyage through the Channel and around the British isles.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodríguez-Salgado1991161-128) These figures do not include eight that were unaccounted for.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodríguez-Salgado1990461–478-129)Other historians have done further research; Neil Hanson, Robert Hutchinson, Colin Martin and Geoffrey Parker all researched the armada ships that had returned, coming to the same conclusion of between 44 and 51 ships being lost overall, with more detailed breakdowns in the number of ships that set sail along with their fate.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutchinson2013276–288-130)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1960228–230-131) That figure represents a third of the fleet having been sunk, captured, wrecked, or scuttled.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999258–259-132) American historian [Garrett Mattingly](/wiki/Garrett_Mattingly "Garrett Mattingly") noted that only 66 ships returned to Spain, with another returning later in the year.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly2005367-133) The losses did not include the smaller vulnerable ships like the pataches and zarbas,[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutchinson2013202,_276–288-134) of which around seventeen were lost.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodríguez-Salgado1990474-135) Even though most of the ships had returned, many of them were severely damaged from either the storms or English gunfire. One hulk, the *Doncella*, sank after they had cast anchor in Santander, and the *Santa Anna* was accidentally burned within a few days of entering San Sebastian.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1960230-136) In addition the severely damaged galleons *San Marcos* and *San Francisco* were broken up, the guns and the timber being sold off. As many as half of the fleet were unfit for further service and as a result a number were scuttled, broken up or left to rot.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly2005333-137)

Furthermore, Spanish sources state that no more than 11,000 perished.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGracia_Rivas1988320-138)[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorrochategui_Santos2018246-139)[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernández_Duro1884138-140) Philip's administrators, bureaucrats and secretaries documented, dated and filed everything that went on in all corners of the Spanish empire, and all those records are still kept in the [National Archives of Spain](/wiki/National_Archives_of_Spain "National Archives of Spain") and the Escorial. The number of men lost was extracted from the paymaster distribution lists.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFernández_Duro1884137–138-141) A detailed analysis of the human cost of the campaign reveals that 25,696 men left Coruña and 13,399 returned. The lowest estimate is 9,000 dead.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGracia_Rivas1991212-20)

Even after arrival the men were near death from disease, as the conditions were very cramped, and most of the ships had run out of food and water. More armada survivors later died in Spain or on hospital ships in Spanish harbours from diseases contracted during the voyage. A large number of prominent Spanish commanders also died, many after having arrived in port. Vice Admiral of the fleet and commander of the Guipuzcoa Squadron, Miguel de Oquendo, suffering from battle wounds and a fever, died at Coruña two days after arriving. Another was the Biscayan squadron commander Juan Martínez de Recalde, who also succumbed the same way.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHardy1966168-142) The Duke of Medina Sidonia also fell ill on his return and nearly succumbed; he was not blamed by Philip, who allowed him to return home to convalesce. Hutchinson claimed that the number of survivors was just over 50%, but these numbers do not include the Portuguese, the Neapolitans and the galley slaves;[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutchinson2013203–204-143) while Hanson claimed that fewer than 10,000 men (38%) survived the expedition.[[f]](#cite_note-144)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanson2011563-22)

### English Counter-Armada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: English Counter-Armada")]

Main articles: [English Armada](/wiki/English_Armada "English Armada") and [List of ships of the English Armada](/wiki/List_of_ships_of_the_English_Armada "List of ships of the English Armada")

The following year the English with Dutch conscripts launched the [Counter-Armada](/wiki/English_Armada "English Armada") under Sir Francis Drake and [Sir John Norris](/wiki/John_Norris_(soldier) "John Norris (soldier)") with three tasks:

* Destroy the battered Spanish Atlantic fleet, which was being repaired in ports of northern Spain
* Make a landing at [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), Portugal, and raise a revolt there against King Philip II (Philip I of Portugal) installing the pretender [Dom António, Prior of Crato](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio,_Prior_of_Crato "António, Prior of Crato") to the Portuguese throne
* Take the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores") if possible so as to establish a permanent base.

The expedition ended in a heavy defeat and none of the objectives were achieved.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorrochategui_Santos2018245–246-145)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBucholzKey2009145-146)[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHampden1972254-147) Dozens of ships were lost, thousands of English soldiers and sailors died, and heavy economic losses were incurred, in a similar disaster to the Spanish Armada.[[142]](#cite_note-148) The attempt to restore the Portuguese Crown from Spain was unsuccessful, and the opportunity to strike a decisive blow against the weakened Spanish Navy was lost. The expedition depleted the financial resources of England's treasury, which had been carefully restored during the long reign of Elizabeth I. Through this lost opportunity, Philip II's naval power marked a revival through the next decade.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElliott1982333-149) The following year he sent 37 ships with 6,420 men to [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany") where they established a [base of operations](/wiki/Brittany_campaign_(1590%E2%80%931598) "Brittany campaign (1590–1598)") on the [Blavet](/wiki/Blavet "Blavet") river. The English and Dutch ultimately failed to disrupt the various fleets of the Indies despite the great number of military personnel mobilized every year.

### Course of the war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Course of the war")]

During the course of the war, the Spanish struggled to gain control of the English Channel or stop the English intervention in Flanders or English privateer transatlantic raids. From their base in Brittany, Spanish corsairs did make several incursions on the English coast and plundered English and Dutch ships.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorrochategui_Santos2018253-150) The Spanish launched a number of small scale attacks such as the [Raid on Mount's Bay](/wiki/Raid_on_Mount%27s_Bay "Raid on Mount's Bay") in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") on 26 July 1595, where over two days, [Penzance](/wiki/Penzance "Penzance"), [Newlyn](/wiki/Newlyn "Newlyn"), [Mousehole](/wiki/Mousehole "Mousehole"), and [Paul](/wiki/Paul,_Cornwall "Paul, Cornwall") were raided and torched. Another smaller [raid on Cawsand bay](/wiki/Attack_on_Cawsand "Attack on Cawsand"), also in Cornwall, took place the following year but ended in failure.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorrochategui_Santos2018258–259-151) In June 1596, England and the United Dutch Provinces sent a [second armada](/wiki/Capture_of_C%C3%A1diz "Capture of Cádiz") to Spain, where they seized and held Cadiz for two weeks causing economic losses, but failed to seize the treasure fleet. After this, three more armadas were sent by Spain – the [second sent in 1596](/wiki/2nd_Spanish_Armada "2nd Spanish Armada") (126–140 ships) was scattered by a storm, as was [the third sent the following year](/wiki/3rd_Spanish_Armada "3rd Spanish Armada") (140 ships), with a number captured or sunk by the English fleet. The [last Armada](/wiki/4th_Spanish_Armada "4th Spanish Armada") (33 ships) sent in October 1601 to Ireland, ended with the [surrender at Kinsale](/wiki/Siege_of_Kinsale "Siege of Kinsale") three months later.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETenace2003855–882-152) The conflict subsequently wound down with diminishing military actions, finally ending with the signing of the [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1604) "Treaty of London (1604)") in August 1604.

## Technological revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Technological revolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Vroom_Hendrick_Cornelisz_Battle_between_England_and_Spain_1601.jpg/250px-Vroom_Hendrick_Cornelisz_Battle_between_England_and_Spain_1601.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vroom_Hendrick_Cornelisz_Battle_between_England_and_Spain_1601.jpg)

Day seven of the battle with the armada, 7 August 1588, by Hendrick Cornelisz Vroom 1601

The Spanish had 117 ships to go up against more than 200 English ships. The opposing forces were experienced in completely different fighting styles. The Spanish style can be studied from the [Battle of Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto"). Their tactics were to fire one cannon volley, ram and grapple the enemy ship, board, then engage in hand-to-hand combat. In contrast, the English style was taking advantage of the wind (the "weather gage") and line-to-line cannon fire from windward, which exposed the opponent ship's hull and rudder as targets. Also instilled was the use of naval cannon to damage enemy ships without the need to board. Until then, the cannon had played a supporting role to the main tactic of ramming and boarding enemy ships.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999201-153) The failure of the Spanish Armada vindicated the English strategy and caused a revolution in naval tactics. The English also had the advantage of fighting close to home, whence they could be easily and frequently resupplied so as not to be weighed down, unlike the armada ships which were loaded with all the [materiel](/wiki/Materiel "Materiel") needed for their invasion force to wage a ground-based war. Nevertheless, when the fleets actually came to blows at the [Battle of Gravelines](/wiki/Battle_of_Gravelines_(1588) "Battle of Gravelines (1588)"), the armada was outnumbered 10:1,[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasado_Soto1991119-72) and during the 8-hour fight, the English managed to sink one carrack and forced two galleons, a pinnace and an armed merchant to run aground. Despite those odds, not once did the armada turn away from a fight; each time it challenged the English fleet, the latter raised its sails to keep its distance.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGorrochategui_Santos201827-154) After the final engagement with the English fleet, the Spanish fleet sailed away, retaining its ability to effectively wage war.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999213–214-155)

Most military historians hold that the battle of Gravelines reflected a lasting shift in the balance of naval power in favour of the English, in part because of the gap in naval technology and cannon armament which continued into the next century.[[150]](#cite_note-156) In the words of historian [Geoffrey Parker](/wiki/Geoffrey_Parker_(historian) "Geoffrey Parker (historian)"), by 1588, "the capital ships of the Elizabethan navy constituted the most powerful battlefleet afloat anywhere in the world".[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1996269–300-157) The English navy yards were leaders in technical innovation, and the captains devised new battle formations and tactics. The sleeker and more manoeuvrable [full-rigged ship](/wiki/Full-rigged_ship "Full-rigged ship"), with ample cannon, was one of the greatest advances of the century and permanently transformed naval warfare.

English shipwrights introduced novel designs, first demonstrated in the [*Foresight*](/wiki/English_ship_Foresight_(1570) "English ship Foresight (1570)") in 1570 and the [*Dreadnought*](/wiki/English_ship_Dreadnought_(1573) "English ship Dreadnought (1573)") in 1573, that allowed the ships to sail faster, manoeuvre better, and carry more and heavier guns.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1996269–300-157) Whereas before warships had tried to grapple with each other so soldiers could board the enemy ship, they were able to stand off and fire broadside cannonades that could sink the vessel. English ships and seamanship had foiled the invasion. The English also benefited from Spain's unworkable strategy that required coordination between the invasion fleet and the Spanish army on shore.[[g]](#cite_note-159) The outdated design of the Spanish cannon meant that they were much slower in reloading in a close-range battle, allowing the English to take control. Spain still had numerically larger fleets, but England was catching up.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker198826–33-160)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Legacy")]

In England, the battle was followed by the distribution of flyers, pamphlets, the striking of victory medals, and numerous joyous celebrations.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutchinson2013217–224-161)[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker1999253–254-162)[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaltby197175–87-163) The victory prompted a huge [David vs Goliath](/wiki/Goliath#Modern_usage_of_"David_and_Goliath" "Goliath") propaganda offensive,[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaltby197175-164) and its exploitation boosted national pride which lasted for years. Elizabeth's legend persisted and grew long after her death. It also may have given heart to the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") cause across Europe and the belief that God was behind the Protestants.: [[h]](#cite_note-166) This was shown by the striking of commemorative medals that bore variations on the inscription, "*1588. Flavit Jehovah et Dissipati Sunt*" – with ["Jehovah" in Hebrew letters](/wiki/Tetragrammaton "Tetragrammaton") ('God blew, and they are scattered'), or '[He blew with His winds, and they were scattered](/wiki/He_blew_with_His_winds,_and_they_were_scattered "He blew with His winds, and they were scattered")'. There were also more lighthearted medals struck, such as the one with the play on [the words](/wiki/Veni,_vidi,_vici "Veni, vidi, vici") of [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar"): *Venit, Vidit, Fugit* ('he came, he saw, he fled'). The wind that scattered the armada has been called *the [Protestant Wind](/wiki/Protestant_Wind "Protestant Wind")*,[[159]](#cite_note-167) a phrase also used for later invasions of England that failed.

The memory of the victory over the armada was evoked during both the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") and the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), when Britain again faced a substantial danger of foreign invasion. During the [Battle of Britain](/wiki/Battle_of_Britain "Battle of Britain") RAF fighter pilots attracted world attention as the "new Elizabethans".[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampion20167-168) The [Armada Memorial](/wiki/Armada_Memorial "Armada Memorial") in Plymouth was constructed in 1888 to celebrate the tercentenary of the defeat of the Spanish Armada.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorth189051–54-169)

One of the greatest finds of the Spanish Armada was the remains of the wreck of *La Girona*, found by a team of [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") divers off the coast of [Portballintrae](/wiki/Portballintrae "Portballintrae") in 1968. It was the greatest treasure salvaged up until that time.[[162]](#cite_note-170) Gold and silver coins, jewelry, armaments and other objects are on permanent display at the [Ulster Museum](/wiki/Ulster_Museum "Ulster Museum") (part of the National Museums of Northern Ireland) in [Stranmillis](/wiki/Stranmillis "Stranmillis") in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast").
