## Historiography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Historiography")]

For 150 years, writers relied heavily on [Augustine Ryther](/wiki/Augustine_Ryther "Augustine Ryther")'s translation of [Petruccio Ubaldini](/wiki/Petruccio_Ubaldini "Petruccio Ubaldini")'s *Expeditionis Hispaniorum in Angliam vera Descriptio* (*A discourse concerninge the Spanishe fleete inuadinge Englande in the yeare 1588*) (1590),[[163]](#cite_note-171) which argued that God decisively favoured the Protestant cause. In the 17th century, [William Camden](/wiki/William_Camden "William Camden") additionally pointed to elements of English nationalism and the private enterprise of the sea dogs. He also emphasized that the Duke of Medina Sidonia was an incompetent seaman.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the 18th century, [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume") praised the leadership of Queen Elizabeth.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, the Whig historians, led by [James A. Froude](/wiki/James_Anthony_Froude "James Anthony Froude"), rejected Hume's interpretation and argued that Elizabeth was vacillating and almost lost the conflict by her unwillingness to spend enough to maintain and supply the Royal Navy's fleet of ships.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Scientific modern historiography came of age with the publication of two volumes of primary documents by [John Knox Laughton](/wiki/John_Knox_Laughton "John Knox Laughton") in 1894. This enabled the leading naval scholar of the day, [Julian Corbett](/wiki/Julian_Corbett "Julian Corbett"), to reject the Whig views and turn attention to the professionalization of the Royal Navy as a critical factor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Twentieth-century historians have focused on technical issues, such as the comparative power of English and Spanish naval guns and the degree of credit for naval battle tactics that is owed to Francis Drake and Charles Howard. Inclement weather in the English Channel and on the oceans at the time has always been cited as a major factor to the outcome. Historian Knerr has reviewed the main trends in [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") over five centuries.[[164]](#cite_note-172)

## Notable participants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Notable participants")]

* [William Adams](/wiki/William_Adams_(pilot) "William Adams (pilot)") served on the *Richarde Dyffylde*, a resupply ship during the campaign. In 1600, he was the first Englishman to reach (and settle in) [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") via the Dutch [United East India Company (VOC)](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") becoming one of the first of few Western [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai").[[165]](#cite_note-173)
* [Lope de Vega](/wiki/Lope_de_Vega "Lope de Vega"), one of the key figures in the Spanish Golden Age of Baroque literature, and one of the most prolific authors in the history of literature, served in the ship *San Juan* during the Spanish Armada.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Spanish_Armada&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The armada has often featured in fictional accounts of the reign of Elizabeth I. Examples are:

* The Battle of Gravelines and the subsequent chase around the northern coast of Scotland form the climax of [Charles Kingsley](/wiki/Charles_Kingsley "Charles Kingsley")'s 1855 novel [*Westward Ho!*](/wiki/Westward_Ho!_(novel) "Westward Ho! (novel)"), which in 1925 became the first novel to be adapted into a radio drama by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC").[[166]](#cite_note-174)
* John Brunner's 1962 science fiction novel *[Times Without Number](/wiki/Times_Without_Number "Times Without Number")* depicts an alternate history where the Spanish Armada prevailed against England, leading to a global Spanish empire.
* The fifth episode of the BBC series *[Elizabeth R](/wiki/Elizabeth_R "Elizabeth R")* (1971) is an account of the defeat of the armada.
* The 2007 film *[Elizabeth: The Golden Age](/wiki/Elizabeth:_The_Golden_Age "Elizabeth: The Golden Age")* contains a heavily fictionalized retelling of the Spanish Armada and the Battle of Gravelines.
* In the 2017 novel *[A Column of Fire](/wiki/A_Column_of_Fire "A Column of Fire")* where the expedition is chronicled through viewpoints that shift between English and Spanish characters.
